Vote.title: Vote
Vote.description: Represents a single vote cast in an election.

id.description: Unique identifier for the vote entity.

timestamp.description: The date and time when the vote was cast.

isVerified.description: Indicates whether the vote has been verified.

id.description: Unique identifier for the voter entity (Firebase Auth UID).

voterId.description: The voter's unique identification string used for login.

firstName.description: The first name of the voter.

lastName.description: The last name of the voter.

voterIdProofHash.description: Hash of the voter ID proof document.

faceImageHash.description: Hash of the voter's facial image.

isAdmin.description: Indicates whether the user has administrative privileges.

Candidate.title: Candidate
Candidate.description: Represents a candidate in an election.

id.description: Unique identifier for the candidate entity.

party.description: The political party of the candidate.

id.description: Unique identifier for the election entity.

startDate.description: The start date and time of the election.

endDate.description: The end date and time of the election.

candidateIds.description: References to Candidates participating in this election. (Relationship: Election 1:N Candidate)

definition.description: Stores individual votes cast by a voter.  Authorization is based on path ownership (voterId matches userId).

params.name: userId
params.description: The ID of the voter who owns the votes.

params.name: voteId
params.description: The ID of the vote document.

definition.description: Stores the details of the user. The user owns this document, authorization is based on path ownership.
Stores information about an election. Access controlled by admin roles.
Stores information about a candidate.  Readable by anyone.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore data structure is designed to support a secure and transparent voting system, OOTU, adhering to the principles of authorization independence, clarity, and QAPs (Rules are not filters). It leverages path-based ownership for user-specific data and denormalization to ensure security rules are simple, robust, and easily debuggable. The structure also facilitates the implementation of security rules that enforce vote anonymity and prevent unauthorized data access, aligning with the application's core features.

**Authorization Independence:**  Authorization independence is achieved primarily by using path-based ownership for votes scoped to a specific voter. There's no need to `get()` parent document data to determine authorization.  Each vote is owned by the voter who cast it, making access control straightforward and atomic. Furthermore, election metadata, is used on the client side to decide on available parameters for an election, and is validated using a collection group query that includes electionId, candidateId, and voterId. This approach eliminates hierarchical authorization dependencies, simplifies security rules, and supports atomic operations (transactions/batches).

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** The structure supports secure `list` operations by ensuring that all documents within a collection share the same security posture. For example, all votes under `/users/{userId}/votes/{voteId}` are owned by the user identified by `{userId}`. Thus listing votes for a specific user is safe.

**Denormalization:** We are implicitly relying on client-side denormalization for operations related to Elections. This is secure because the rules only allow the document to be created, the rules do not allow the document to be read or updated, which means that all reads must happen through a server.

**Election Design Notes:** Elections are used as global entities. The rules for this collection are secured to only allow admin roles to create elections.
